_note: Per-specialist case material for Creative. Used at workerNode (per specialty turn), not at recommendation. The specialist retrieves 2-3 cases whose `business_type_category` matches the inferred category (or `concept_first` when no business type is inferred — typical for concept-first openers), and weaves the commit-and-exclude insight into the turn without naming the source — per CLAUDE.md GR#6 ("use the case, don't cite it"). Every case here exhibits Creative's domain discipline: name the one angle to commit to AND the nearby truth to rule out. A case that commits without excluding is optionality in disguise; a case that excludes without committing is critique in disguise. Creative owns the commitment shape.

cases.challenge_pattern: brand or concept name the owner is attached to but which carries meaning that doesn't match what the business actually is
cases.observation: Small-batch hot sauce producer whose working name for three years had been something that signaled "hottest" — a name the founder liked, had designed labels around, and had used on Instagram. Early reviews and interviews kept returning to the same reaction: "wait, so it's not actually the hottest?" The sauces were medium-heat with complex flavor profiles built around fruit, fermentation, and smoke. Every time the name created an expectation, the product disappointed on that expectation — even when customers loved what they actually got. The owner's attachment to the name was costing reviews and repeat purchases; the product's real story was flavor depth, not heat.
cases.what_worked: Treating the name as a positioning problem, not an identity one. The owner renamed the brand around the smoke-and-fruit story and rebuilt the label accordingly. The product didn't change; the promise did. Review language shifted within six weeks from heat-complaints to flavor-praise. The exclusion — ruling out "hottest" as a category the brand had no intention of competing in — freed the marketing to talk about what the brand was actually good at. Commit to the flavor angle; rule out the heat-warrior category that was silently setting the wrong expectation.
cases.what_wasted_money: Two years of Instagram content that leaned into heat-warrior imagery because "that's what the name implied." The audience built on that imagery self-selected for heat-seeking customers who bounced off the actual flavor profile. The name had built the wrong audience at the top of the funnel.
cases.one_line_lesson: A name that tells customers what the thing isn't does the brand's work against itself. Commit to what the product actually is; exclude the category it was never going to win in.

cases.challenge_pattern: owner treats a single feature or tactic as if it is the whole business
cases.observation: Early-stage founder pitching a browser extension that would auto-apply coupon codes at checkout. The pitch kept describing "the extension" as if it were the business. Questioned further, the founder couldn't say who the end user was beyond "people who shop online," how the business would make money, or what would happen once Honey and Rakuten already did this at scale with million-user distribution. The extension was a feature. The business — which audience, which retailers, which revenue mechanic, which moat — was undefined behind the feature. Building the extension was the easy part; the founder had collapsed it into "the idea" and stopped there.
cases.what_worked: Separating the feature from the business. The extension stayed — it was capable engineering. But the actual business question became: for whom is this feature more valuable than Honey, specifically? The answer, after three hours of conversation, was that beauty-industry resellers operating on small margins were Honey-invisible and spent hours per week stacking codes manually. That was a business — a vertical extension plus a community layer plus a referral revenue model. The commitment was the vertical; the exclusion was the general-shopper audience that had been implicit in the original pitch. The extension was the wedge; the vertical was the business.
cases.what_wasted_money: Three months of the founder adding features to the general-purpose extension (auto-apply, stacking detection, reminders) before the business question was even asked. Each feature competed directly with Honey's engineering team and added nothing distinctive to justify the switch. The feature arms race was a dead end that the business thinking — had it come first — would have avoided.
cases.one_line_lesson: A feature becomes a business when it answers "for whom specifically, at what cost to them, with what that others can't match." A feature without those answers is a tech demo with optimism attached.

cases.challenge_pattern: concept-first opener where the mechanic and the feeling it produces have been conflated into a single design layer
cases.observation: Solo designer with a prototype of a resource-constrained dungeon crawler — the player is assigned a limited number of placement pieces to traverse a dungeon floor, runs out, and gets stranded. Playtest feedback was polite but not excited. The designer's instinct was that the mechanic needed more variety or the dungeon more decoration. The real question was different: what the designer had actually built was a spatial puzzle where the evidence of a bad choice is permanently visible on the floor. The pieces weren't the game — the regret was the game. The mechanic and the feeling had been collapsed into one layer and the designer was iterating on the wrong one.
cases.what_worked: Naming the feeling first — the game is spatial regret made visible; the pieces are one carrier for it — and asking which side to build on. If the feeling was the point, the specific piece mechanic was negotiable, and the real design question was how to make the regret hit harder. If the mechanic was the point, the feeling was a consequence and the puzzle layout carried the design weight. The commitment was to regret-as-game; the exclusion was pieces-as-game. That exclusion freed the next six weeks of prototyping to iterate on the layer that actually made the concept distinct.
cases.what_wasted_money: Three months of the designer adding piece types, colors, and variants because the polite playtest feedback read as "mechanic isn't varied enough." None of it moved the excitement needle because none of it made the regret feel more real. The mechanic layer had been optimized; the feeling layer had been ignored.
cases.one_line_lesson: The mechanic carries the feeling; it is not the feeling. Name which layer is the game, and the other becomes a craft choice — not the design question itself.

cases.challenge_pattern: product concept where the "what" keeps drifting into an adjacent product the owner is half-imagining
cases.observation: Founder pitching an app for parents of toddlers — short guided sessions built around the two-minute windows that exist between tantrums, bath time, and the end of a nap. Each time they described the product, the description drifted: sometimes it was a meditation app, sometimes a parenting-advice app with meditations inside, sometimes a sleep-tracker for kids with a parent-breathing feature. Three products wearing the same UI. The prototype screens mixed elements from all three. Prospective users trying to explain it back to the founder came away with three different summaries. The founder thought the problem was unclear copy; the real problem was that the product hadn't committed to which of the three it was.
cases.what_worked: Forcing the founder to answer one question: if a tired parent opens this app at 7:12pm in the middle of a bath-time meltdown and only has ninety seconds, what is the one thing this app does for them? The answer was the two-minute meditation — the parenting advice and the sleep tracking were adjacent possibilities the founder was keeping open in case the meditation "wasn't enough." The commitment to meditation-only, and the explicit ruling out of the other two, shrank the product and made the first thirty seconds of using it immediately legible. The other two were real product ideas — they were just not this one.
cases.what_wasted_money: Six months of design iterations that tried to serve all three products simultaneously. The home screen had three tabs because the founder couldn't pick. Each feature area had compromises that served neither the meditation use case cleanly nor the parenting-advice use case cleanly. The optionality was written into the interface.
cases.one_line_lesson: A concept that fits inside two sentences for the user has committed. A concept that keeps drifting across adjacent products hasn't — and the drift is visible to everyone except the founder.

cases.challenge_pattern: premium product in a commodity category leans on features (ingredients, craft, quality) instead of the specific reason a buyer pays a steep multiple of the commodity price
cases.observation: Small-batch candle brand at $38 per candle, real quality in ingredients (premium wax blend, proprietary scent notes), beautiful packaging. Product-detail pages listed ingredients, burn time, and fragrance notes — the same spec shape as a $12 commodity candle, just with fancier words. The actual reason the brand's repeat customers bought was not ingredients. It was that the candles were forever-gift-quality — the specific thing a gift-giver could arrive with at a hostess dinner and have the hostess feel seen without the giver having to know the recipient well. It was an identity-signaling gift mechanic. Nothing on the site named that. The pages read as if the brand were competing on features with the commodity tier.
cases.what_worked: Rebuilding the category pages around the gift mechanic. The scent and ingredient copy moved below the fold. The top of each page spoke to the gift-giver's question — what to arrive with tonight — and committed to an answer: the candle that says you paid attention. Conversion rate on landing pages roughly doubled within two months. The commitment was to the gift-moment; the exclusion was the ingredient-list framing that had been matching the commodity. Ruling out the feature-match framing was the move that let the actual use case surface.
cases.what_wasted_money: Twelve months of Meta and Google Shopping spend targeting "premium candles" and "soy candle gifts" — category searches that put the brand side-by-side with every commodity option on price, where the $38 price was a liability. The audience finding them through the feature-match funnel was the wrong audience.
cases.one_line_lesson: In a commodity category, features are price-comparison bait. The angle is the specific use-case the buyer is hiring the product for — the reason they would not switch to the cheap alternative even when the features read identically.

cases.challenge_pattern: owner positions around category phrases ("results-driven," "personalized programming") that every competitor also uses
cases.observation: Solo personal trainer in a metro market competing against twelve other independents plus two chain gyms with in-house trainers. His Instagram bio said "results-driven personalized training." So did most of the other twelve. What was different about him — the thing his retained clients said again and again — was that he refused to program cardio for clients whose actual goal was strength, even when clients asked for it, and he was firm about sleep before he was firm about workouts. The opinionated posture was the business. It was not "personalized" — it was specific, and it cost him clients who wanted a hype-responsive trainer.
cases.what_worked: Rewriting the bio as a specific commitment: strength-first programming; he will not sell cardio when the client came for strength; sleep is the first conversation. The language sounded narrow — and was. Follower count fell; inquiry count rose; close rate on inquiries roughly doubled because the prospects who reached out had already self-selected for the posture. Commit to the opinionated posture; rule out "results-driven" and "personalized" — the category phrases that had been flattening him into the interchangeable-trainer bucket.
cases.what_wasted_money: Roughly eighteen months of client churn where clients arrived expecting hype-responsive programming, got frustrated by the strength-first insistence, and left. The category-phrase marketing had been bringing in the wrong clients; the retention problem was actually a positioning problem.
cases.one_line_lesson: Trainer credentials and adjectives are all interchangeable. The angle is the specific posture you'd rather lose a sale than compromise on — and that posture has to be named on the page before the client arrives.

cases.challenge_pattern: the studio's philosophy is the real differentiator but customer-facing copy leads with scheduling logistics
cases.observation: Boutique yoga studio whose founding teacher had trained for fifteen years in a specific lineage and whose studio's philosophy — prop-heavy, slow, alignment-first — was materially different from the other two yoga studios in town. The homepage led with a "Book a class" button and a schedule grid. Below the fold, after four logistic blocks, was a paragraph about the founder's lineage and what made the practice distinct. Newcomers treating the site like a gym-website looked at prices and times and compared to the other studios — all of which were cheaper. The studio's actual buyer — someone who had outgrown power-flow classes and was looking for something more grounded — couldn't tell from above the fold why this place was different.
cases.what_worked: Flipping the page. The top became a plain statement of the practice: slow, prop-heavy, alignment-first; not power flow; for people with injuries, recovered from injuries, or who had outgrown faster classes. The schedule moved down. Conversion rate doubled on organic traffic — the visitors who stayed were the ones the copy had already spoken to. Commit to the philosophy as the first thing the site says; rule out the "friendly neighborhood yoga studio" positioning that had been pulling the site into a category comparison where price was the only distinguishing axis.
cases.what_wasted_money: Two years of Google Ads targeting "yoga near me" broad match. The clicks came; the conversions were terrible because the site read as yet another studio, and the studio's real buyer wasn't arriving through category search.
cases.one_line_lesson: If the philosophy is the differentiator, the philosophy has to be on the first screen. Scheduling logistics compete on price; philosophy competes on fit — and fit is a higher-margin conversion.

cases.challenge_pattern: business leans on category phrases ("quality craftsmanship," "built to last") that every competitor also uses
cases.observation: Two-person custom cabinetry shop whose website said "quality craftsmanship, built to last, attention to detail." So did the websites of the eight other shops in the metro. Customers couldn't tell them apart from the page. What actually distinguished this shop — what the owner's best-retained clients mentioned repeatedly — was that the lead carpenter designed joinery that didn't require face screws. Everything was hidden. It was a specific, provable, forwardable craft decision. The owner hadn't put it on the page because "that's just how I work; everyone in my trade does that." Everyone in his trade did not; his last three competitor-shop projects he'd been called to fix had been full of visible screws.
cases.what_worked: Replacing the category adjectives with the specific commitment: no visible fasteners. A twelve-word promise that separated the shop from the entire category. Quoted jobs rose 30% in the next six months; the buyers who arrived were specifically looking for the craft call the website now named. The commitment was to the specific provable joinery choice; the exclusion was "quality craftsmanship" and "attention to detail" — off the page entirely, because leaving them on the page was the move that had been flattening the shop into the indistinguishable cluster.
cases.what_wasted_money: Three years of Google Ads spending on "quality cabinetry" keyword bids that competed with every shop in the metro at roughly identical cost per click. The generic copy had no angle for the traffic to latch onto; conversion was proportional to spend only, not to fit.
cases.one_line_lesson: Any adjective every competitor could also claim is wallpaper. The angle is the specific craft call your best clients already notice — and the category word your marketing is hiding behind.

cases.challenge_pattern: owner's marketing speaks to peers in the trade rather than to the overwhelmed buyers the business serves
cases.observation: Solo home organizer whose Instagram had 3,000 followers — most of them other organizers, stagers, and lifestyle bloggers. The grid was pristine — perfectly folded laundry, labeled pantry jars, color-coded bookshelves. Her target buyer was an overwhelmed working parent of young children. That buyer, scrolling her feed, felt shame rather than recognition — "I could never keep it looking like that." The grid was selling the aesthetic of someone who had already solved the problem to an audience still inside it. The organizer kept optimizing for engagement metrics that were coming from peers, not prospects.
cases.what_worked: Rebuilding the feed around the messy middle — before photos, decision-points, how to choose what to donate when you're overwhelmed, the hardest drawer to open in your house and why. The grid stopped being pretty. DMs from overwhelmed parents jumped within six weeks; bookings followed. Commit to the audience still inside the problem; rule out the aspirational-clean-home aesthetic that had been making peers engage and prospects recoil. The exclusion was uncomfortable — peers stopped liking her posts — but was the commitment that made the marketing work for its actual audience.
cases.what_wasted_money: Four years of content calibrated to peer-audience engagement. The metric that mattered (DM from a prospective client) was roughly zero for most of that time despite the engagement numbers looking healthy.
cases.one_line_lesson: If the people liking your posts are not the people who would pay for your work, your audience is wrong. Commit to the audience you can help; rule out the audience that makes you feel validated.

cases.challenge_pattern: consultant in a crowded category reaches for the category phrase instead of the specific posture clients already credit them with
cases.observation: Solo AI consultant with a product-management background whose retained clients all described her the same way after a year: "she told me when AI wasn't the answer." On her website, she was "an AI consultant helping businesses unlock the power of AI." The website was indistinguishable from every other AI consultant in the category. The actual thing her clients valued — that she pushed back on bad AI projects and steered them toward simpler, cheaper solutions — was the one thing her marketing was not saying. She was afraid that naming it would position her as "anti-AI" and lose prospects who were excited about AI.
cases.what_worked: Committing to the angle her clients already described: the translator clients came to specifically because she would tell them when AI was NOT worth doing. The website headline became a version of that — specific enough that prospects who wanted a hype-man self-selected out, and prospects who wanted judgment self-selected in. The commitment was to the translator posture; the exclusion was "AI evangelist" and "full-service AI implementation partner" — the two positionings she had been leaving available because she feared narrowing the pipeline. Narrowing the pipeline was the move.
cases.what_wasted_money: Eighteen months of content and outbound targeting AI-curious executives with category messaging. The conversions were low-quality: half the prospects who booked a call were looking for someone to validate a project the consultant would end up telling them not to do, and the mismatch burned intro calls.
cases.one_line_lesson: In a crowded category, the thing your best clients already credit you with is the angle — even when (especially when) it sounds like it excludes half the category. The exclusion IS the commitment.

cases.challenge_pattern: senior operator's marketing leans on credentials and experience instead of the specific thing they do differently
cases.observation: Senior management consultant with 20 years at top-tier firms, launching an independent practice. The website had five paragraphs of credentials — firms, titles, industries, marquee clients. What the site didn't say was the one thing that actually got her hired: her willingness to tell founding teams that their entire strategic direction was wrong, in week one. Every client who stayed described a version of that moment. Her peers in the same category had similar credentials. Her differentiator — the hard posture early — was not a credential, and she was uncomfortable putting it on the page because it sounded arrogant.
cases.what_worked: Replacing the credentials-stack with one specific promise: the first engagement deliverable would name the strategic drift the client had been avoiding. The credentials moved to a footer. Close rate on qualified inbound moved from 20% to 48%; the prospects who booked calls after the rewrite arrived already expecting the hard posture. Commit to the posture; rule out the "world-class strategic advisory with deep industry expertise" headline that had been framing her as a peer-of-big-firm alternative competing on price and credentials.
cases.what_wasted_money: Eight months of content marketing positioned as a peer-of-Deloitte alternative on price and credentials. The prospects it attracted were price-shopping between her and the big firms, rarely chose her, and when they did were looking for big-firm deliverables she refused to produce.
cases.one_line_lesson: Senior credentials are table stakes in the category. The angle is the specific thing you do differently from peers with identical credentials — usually the thing you've been afraid to say on the page because it narrows the audience.

cases.challenge_pattern: positioning relies on category labels the buyer can't place; the mechanic the buyer recognizes is the real angle
cases.observation: Fractional CFO priced at $2K/month whose close rate was stuck at 15% despite strong inbound volume. The site led with "fractional CFO services for growing businesses" — a category phrase the ideal buyer (a founder approaching their first board meeting) didn't search for and didn't recognize. When the same founder later described the service in their own words after signing, it was always some version of: "you gave me the monthly financial picture I needed to walk into a board meeting and not feel like an idiot." That was the buyer's mechanic — what to say when a board member asks a financial question I can't answer — and the site never spoke to it.
cases.what_worked: Rewriting the top of the page around the mechanic: the board-meeting readiness, the VC-question prep, the investor-update shape. The category label moved to a footnote. Close rate moved to 38% in the quarter that followed, on the same inbound volume. Commit to the buyer's mechanic as the headline; rule out the profession's category label as the lead — the label was what the profession called it, not what the buyer was searching for.
cases.what_wasted_money: Two years of the category-phrase site copy. Every inbound lead who arrived via category search was partially disqualified by not yet recognizing the urgency; every inbound lead who arrived via a specific VC-question pain point had to read past the category headline to find out the service matched their problem.
cases.one_line_lesson: The angle is the mechanic the buyer recognizes in their own vocabulary. The category label is what the profession calls it — which is rarely what the buyer is thinking when they start looking.

cases.challenge_pattern: the founder's story is the differentiator but the customer-facing surface treats it as an "about" footnote
cases.observation: Neighborhood bakery run by a woman who had inherited her grandmother's handwritten recipe book and spent five years testing every recipe in a modern kitchen before opening. Six of the menu items were direct recipe translations from a specific wartime lineage; customers could not have known that. The menu page listed items with prices and no context. The About page told the grandmother's story beautifully — and got almost no traffic because people scanning a bakery site went to the menu, ordered, and left. The entire point of the bakery — the specific old recipes, the specific lineage — was structurally invisible at the moment of decision.
cases.what_worked: Moving the recipe-lineage annotation onto the menu itself. Four of the items gained a one-line note tying them to the original dated recipe. The About page stayed for people who wanted the full story. The menu-page notes changed the purchase experience — customers chose specifically for the heritage-story items more often, word-of-mouth carried a specific recommendation rather than "the bakery is good." Commit to putting the story on the menu where the decision happens; rule out the menu-as-inventory-list convention that had been hiding the thing that made the bakery worth traveling for.
cases.what_wasted_money: Eighteen months of social content about the grandmother and the recipe book that drove traffic to the About page but rarely converted — the path from story to purchase was broken at the menu page, where the story vanished.
cases.one_line_lesson: If the story is the differentiator, put it on the surface where the decision happens. Story behind an About-page link is story behind glass; story on the menu is story the customer can buy.

cases.challenge_pattern: positioning phrase is a category claim every local competitor also makes
cases.observation: New bistro opening in a small city where four other restaurants already described themselves as "farm to table." The owner's website led with the same phrase. The owner's actual sourcing was genuinely tighter than the others — all produce from within 40 miles, all meat from three named farms he had hunted with personally, menu changes every two weeks with whatever was in peak. The phrase flattened all of that into a category claim diners couldn't distinguish from marketing. In food-critic reviews, the bistro got lumped in with the other four and couldn't rise above.
cases.what_worked: Replacing the category phrase with the specific mechanic: the menu names the farm and the farmer for each protein. The owner's hunting partnerships with three specific families became the headline, with names and faces. The specific-radius produce commitment was stated — everything within forty miles, named on the menu. Critics started writing about the bistro as distinct from the "farm to table" cluster, and the phrase itself dropped from the site entirely. Commit to the named-farm and named-farmer mechanic; rule out the phrase that had been collapsing the real sourcing into a claim diners couldn't verify and couldn't distinguish.
cases.what_wasted_money: Six months of menu printing and marketing material using the generic phrase while the owner wondered why diners didn't "get" the difference. The marketing had collapsed his real commitment into a category claim, and the diners weren't wrong — they couldn't have known from the page.
cases.one_line_lesson: A phrase every local competitor uses is a category claim, not an angle. The angle is the specific mechanic they can't or won't copy — and the category phrase is what the marketing has to rule out to let it land.
